results.title: Day of the dead (D)
results.creator_credit: George A. Romero, Richard Rubinstein, Laurel Productions and United Film Distribution Company
results.brief_description: The third and bleakest of George A. Romero’s ‘Living dead’ series (which consist of ‘Night of the living dead’ and ‘Dawn of the dead’), is also the most goriest. Essentially, society has collapsed and the living dead have taken over the world. The film picks up on a small group of survivors, made up of scientists and a small band of soldiers who have barricaded themselves in an abandoned missile silo. The scientists desperately search for a solution to the zombie plague, while the military become increasingly agitated at their lack of results. Like Romero’s previous outings into the world of the undead, the varied groups are microcosms of human nature and not surprisingly, the most unpleasant characters in this film are not the zombies but the humans themselves. Cast includes Lori Cardille, Terry Alexander, Joe Pilato, Richard Liberty and Howard Sherman.

results.title: Davy Crockett: king of the wild frontier
results.creator_credit: Norman Foster, Bill Walsh and Walt Disney Productions
results.brief_description: Davy Crockett was a legendary folk hero. Starring Fess Parker as Crockett, the hero fights alligators, battles an Indian Chief in a savage tomahawk duel and fights for freedom at the ill-fated Alamo. Accompanied by his friend George Russell, played by Buddy Ebsen, “Davy Crockett” is a daring adventure of drama, humour and excitement and will remain a colourful portrayal of the frontier.

results.title: Our fragile coast
results.creator_credit: David Hughes and Victoria. Audio-Visual Education Centre
results.brief_description: Examines the twin processes of erosion and deposition along the Victorian coast. These areas include Port Cambell, the Gippsland Lakes, Ninety-Mile Beach, and Port Phillip Bay. Sea-Beach poem by Judith Wright.

results.title: Our Dynamic Earth
results.brief_description: Live footage of earthquakes and volcanoes dramatises changes in the earth’s crust. Animation explains these events in terms of the theory of plate tectonics. Many of the plate boundaries are located in deep ocean waters and, in a submersible vehicle scientists explore an underwater ridge where two plates are drifting apart.

results.title: Our church - portrait of a parish
results.creator_credit: Roberta King, Ronald Squire, Canadian Broadcasting Corporation and King Squire Films
results.brief_description: Film of parish life spoken by the Minister and parishioners young and old. Details births and marriages, town fair and parade and fundraising. As with all parishes worldwide, money is always a problem. The church was built in 1885 and renovated inside in 1970.

results.title: Oscar's greatest moments, 1971-1991
results.creator_credit: Jeff Margolis and Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences
results.brief_description: An anthology of Hollywood’s biggest stars, funniest moments, famous speeches, glamourous and outrageous fashions, lavish production numbers, most popular songs, and legendary Honourary Award recipients - 21 years of show-stopping magic and excitement. Hosted by Karl Malden, this extraordinary documentary compiles the most memorable highlights from 21 years of televised Academy Awards.

results.title: David Bowie: serious moonlight
results.creator_credit: David Mallet, Anthony Eaton and Concert Productions International
results.brief_description: Filmed live performance of David Bowie on his 1983 Serious Moonlight World Tour at the P.N.E. Grandstand, Vancouver, British Columbia. Highly regarded and world renowned rock performer/entertainer, David Bowie sings his way through some of his most popular classics.

results.title: Day by day
results.creator_credit: Robin James, ABC-TV (Australia) and Griffith University
results.brief_description: We look at some of the daily activities of the Chinese inhabitants of Beijing, beginning with Tai Chi exercises in the morning. Highlights work work, school, peasant farmers in a collective near Beijing, lunchtime, Children’s palaces where children may indulge in extra-curricular activities in sport and the arts, and Sunday visits to the park and the Forbidden City. Professor Colin Mackerras explains the history of calligraphy, and the ways in which the Chinese spend their time. Two dramatised segments show a grandfather sitting in the park and a family quarrel.

results.title: Dawn of the dead (Uncut version) [DVD] (D)
results.creator_credit: George A. Romero, Richard Rubinstein and Laurel Group Productions
results.brief_description: In this sequel to George Romero’s “Night of the living dead”, the US is engulfed and decimated by a zombie population growing at an inexplicable and alarming rate. Two Philadelphia police S.W.A.T. officers, a helicopter pilot and his TV reporter girlfriend escape the city and take refuge in an abandoned suburban shopping mall. Their attempts to isolate themselves from the marauding army of the undead are thwarted after a band of looters leave a door open allowing the zombies free access to the mall. Romero capitalises on the shopping mall location to present an apocalyptic allegory for a rampant consumer culture driven to eat itself by its own insatiable appetite. Cast includes David Emge, Ken Foree, Scott H. Reininger. Music by Dario Argento.

results.title: Oscar Wilde: spend thrift of genius
results.brief_description: Oscar Wilde, wonderfully outrageous and brilliantly witty, is probably one of Ireland’s most prominent writers. Born in Dublin in 1854 to literary parents, Wilde attended Trinity College where he was educated in the classical studies of art and literature. Wilde went on to study at Oxford where he was deeply influenced by the writings of art critics John Ruskin and Walter Pater. This well put together biographical documentary follows Wilde’s life from childhood through to his successful prominence as a poet and playwright in society London, his philandering with the many beautiful young professional women, his marriage to Constance Lloyd, the eventual realisation of his homosexuality and his subsequent relationship with the very much younger Lord Alfred Douglas. It was as a result of this affair that finally led to Wilde’s incarceration, spending two years in jail over a lost libel suit. When he was finally released in 1897, his popularity had greatly diminished and the “darling” of London society soon exiled himself to France and Italy. His almost overnight downfall resulted in bankruptcy and an inability to revive his literary career. Oscar Wilde died in Paris on 30 November 1900 but his wit, his brilliance as a dramatist (Lady Windermere’s fan, An Ideal husband, The Picture of Dorian Gray, The Importance of being Ernest), his love for the aesthetics of life, and his total disregard for conformity will live on forever. In fact, some of Wilde’s famous quotes are still used today and he has been satirised and characterised by many over the century including Gilbert and Sullivan’s comic opera “Patience” and the periodical Punch. Features archival footage, stills, sites and locations frequented throughout his life including the house he was born in and readings and quotations from his personal letters and writings. Narrated by Denys Hawthorne and voice of Oscar Wilde by Alan Stanford.
